children.text: There was Delores, she was the wife of one of the Berets. There was Virginia, and at that time she was Jerry- my compadre’s- wife, ex-wife now. But (coughs) Basically, it was us. Blanca came along, because Blanca was with Victor. Victor brought her. And that was- that was about it. It was a handful of women.
Why do you think it was so difficult to recruit Chicanas?

First of all, I don't think they were ready to be soldadas. First, number one, you know, I mean, pick up a rifle. We would have been labeled as terrorists today if we would actually have carried out our plans the way we had kind of set them up. And I think we would've, I know we were under the FBI radar. We were under the police department’s radar for sure. And I knew that- and I think that was part of it. And the other part was- we still had to struggle with this “yo lo hombre, tú la mujere." You know, "you're gonna do what I say.” And that was very, even, it was hard for me. And you can ask my big brother about that because he says- you know my mother says- she raised all these men but the aggressive one in that family was me! You know, I was the pelionera. I said I had to grow up competing with you guys so of course I had to go the extra distance. I was ready and very committed, you know to these changes that were supposed to come about, you know, by any means necessary. We marchabamos. We protested. I never got arrested for protesting, though. Just seen by la gracia de dios, you know, that we never did.

One of the positive things that I think came out of that was – uhm his name is Ralphie and he was running for sheriff but I can't remember his last name- Ralph Lopez. He was a sergeant with the police department. Started coming- He knew Juan and he started coming around the headquarters, and we were all anti-police, man! Because, we were- that was our main thing was the police were beating up on these Chicanos, you know I mean for nothing! Just, just- beating them up. Killing them. Shooting them. Arresting them, you know, harassing, you know the young people. Because that was one of the things that we did- we use to tail the police when they were on their beats. You know, and not too many police like that. But we did it. And you know, that's one of the things. Anyway, they started coming around (mumbles). I don't know how he knew Juan, but either way that was the first steps into creating the community relations office with the police department. Because prior to that I do not think they had one. So in a big way they were instrumental.

The Brown Berets were also very instrumental I think in creating these so-called “truce-” "truce," you know, peace, uh war councils- they would call them. You know where they would sit down and have these “peace talks” with these different gangs. (coughs) Did I sit in? I don't remember sitting in in any of those. Were they successful? I'm not sure. I can't tell you. Because I saw a lot of conflict, you know, within the Berets from the different barrios, you know. And so, they had that- that kind of uneasy truce that (coughs) and it was very precarious (sneezes) truce.
Can you tell me about the difference between- what are the differences between the south side and the west side Brown Berets?

You want me to be honest, huh? (Coughs) When Victor and his brother Frank- and I forget who else- (sneezes) right away, they approached Juan and the Brown Berets we were having a meeting. And I’ll never forget, they drove up and Juan told us, “These guys are gonna come over and talk to us. They're from the south side.” There is no love lost between the south side and the west side, you know, it was territorial stuff. They're coming in as a Chicano three. They were just them and whoever else was in their organization wanting to create a south side chapter of the Brown Berets. Something about Victor- I mean we are en paz- but at that time, man, just something about that just made me feel- NO! No no no no no. And I voted no and stuck to my guns. You know, and uh, I was like "No you're gonna live to regret that. And sure enough I think that was the beginning of the end of the west side Berets, you know. I can't exactly tell you all the things that happened, because all of a sudden they were out there doing things that should not have been done.

The Brown Berets were infiltrated by local police departments, the United States Bureau of Alcohol, Firearms, Tobacco, and Explocives (BAFTE/AFT) at every level and were also closely monitored by the FBI at their various chapters across the country.

Women were disueded from joining the Brown Berets because it had an internal culture of "Machismo," which is an extreme form of maculenity present in many Latino cultures. In Machismo men are expected and taught to be domineering, sexist, agressive, and prideful. It is masculinity in excess. Not all Latino men subscribe to Machismo, as masculinity in Latino culture can also be positive, sometimes called "Caballerismo" (Gentlemanism). Caballerismo teaches hard-work, responsiblilty, loyalty and protection of one's family and community, active fatherhood, and respect towards women. The Machismo present in the Brown Berets meant women were often relegated to "feminine" tasks like cooking, cleaning, and clerical work, as well as treating them as unherently unequal. Still, many women fought to be included in the Brown Berets and used their positions within the organization to empower themselves as well as other women.
